name: Cloudflare Workers KV API
what_it_does: Globally distributed, eventually consistent key-value storage layer built on Cloudflare's edge network. KV values are written to a central store and replicated to all edge locations within ~60 seconds. Values can be up to 25 MB. Keys support TTL-based expiration. Accessible from Workers code via bindings (runtime API) or from outside via REST API. Optimized for high-read, low-write workloads like configuration, feature flags, and user session lookups.
use_cases: Storing feature flags and configuration that Workers read on every request with zero latency
Caching API responses at the edge with automatic TTL expiration
User session tokens or auth state accessible globally without origin round-trips
A/B test variant assignments keyed by user ID or cookie value
Static asset metadata or content at the edge as a simple CDN origin layer
not_for: Strongly consistent coordination or locking — use Durable Objects instead
High-write workloads — KV writes are expensive ($5/million) and have eventual consistency
Relational data or anything requiring secondary indexes or queries
best_when: Your workload is read-heavy (many reads per write), data can tolerate ~60 seconds of staleness, and you need globally low-latency reads from inside Cloudflare Workers.
avoid_when: You need read-after-write consistency, high write throughput, or any query capability beyond key lookup. Durable Objects are better for coordination; R2 is better for large objects.

auth.notes: API tokens with Workers KV Storage:Read and Workers KV Storage:Edit permissions. Account ID required in all REST API calls. From inside a Worker, KV is accessed via the binding object (no auth tokens needed at runtime — permissions are set at deployment time).

pricing.free_tier_limits: 100,000 reads/day, 1,000 writes/day, 1,000 deletes/day, 1,000 list operations/day, 1 GB storage free
pricing.paid_tiers: Workers Paid plan ($5/month): 10M reads/month free, then $0.50/million. Writes: $5/million. Storage: $0.50/GB-month beyond included.
pricing.notes: Write costs are 10x read costs — design your data model to minimize writes. Bulk key listing (list operations) counts toward the list quota, not reads. Large-value storage costs accumulate if you store many multi-MB values.

performance.rate_limits: REST API: 1,200 requests per 5 minutes per API token. Runtime KV bindings have no documented per-invocation rate limit but are subject to account-level quotas. Write propagation to all edges: up to 60 seconds.

data_residency: No per-region data residency control for KV (unlike Durable Objects)

agent_readiness.error_message_notes: KV REST API errors follow the standard Cloudflare errors array format with code and message. Missing key returns a 404 with a clear error. Quota exceeded returns a 429 with a clear message. Runtime KV binding errors in Workers surface as exceptions with descriptive messages.
agent_readiness.idempotency_notes: KV put operations are fully idempotent — writing the same key-value pair multiple times produces the same result. Delete of a non-existent key is not an error (returns null). Conditional puts using metadata or ETags are not natively supported.
known_agent_gotchas: KV is eventually consistent — writes propagate to edge nodes within ~60 seconds but there is NO guarantee of read-after-write consistency even on the same edge node; an agent that writes a value and immediately reads it back may get the old value

The KV list() operation returns keys in lexicographic order and uses cursor-based pagination; listing all keys in a large namespace requires multiple API calls — agents that assume a single list() returns everything will miss keys silently

KV values can be up to 25 MB but values larger than ~512 KB begin to impact edge replication speed and read latency noticeably; agents storing large JSON blobs should consider chunking or using R2 instead

Namespaces must be created before use and require the namespace ID (not the name) in all API calls; the namespace ID is assigned by Cloudflare at creation and is not the same as the binding name in your Worker

TTL (expiration) is set at write time and cannot be updated independently without rewriting the value; agents that want to 'refresh' a TTL must re-put the full value with a new expiration, which counts as a write operation for billing

security_score_components.security_notes: Data encrypted at rest. TLS enforced on REST API. API token scopes are granular (read vs write). No per-key ACLs — access is all-or-nothing per namespace. Namespace IDs are not guessable but are not secrets. Data is globally distributed with no regional data residency option.
